value:
Codex entries are seeds, not knowledge stores. They select a region of pretrained-LLM terrain rather than storing the terrain itself. Optimize for activation per byte: a few precise project-specific terms that drop the reader into a dense biome the LLM couldn't have found on its own.

Test for "should I write this entry?" — does this seed land somewhere the LLM couldn't have reached on its own? If no, skip it. If yes, the byte cost is justified.

Bad seed (low amplification): "npm test runs the tests" — terrain the LLM would generate by default.
Good seed (high amplification): "withFileLock fail-closed in production with CODEX_DISABLE_LOCKING=1 opt-out for tests" — specific terrain that only exists because of choices you made.

Frequency telemetry doesn't measure what data is "important" — it measures which seeds drop the agent in dense biomes most often. Hot entries are well-positioned, not necessarily large.

THE METAPHOR:
Minecraft seeds. A small int + a generator → a structured world. Codex entry + LLM → a structured response. Neither input contains the world; both are indices into a generator that already has it. Codex bytes don't smuggle Shannon information past their byte count — they activate context already living in the LLM's pretrained weights.

NICE PROPERTY:
Codex entries appreciate as LLMs improve. Better generator + same seed = bigger world. The work invested today doesn't decay with model versions; it gets more valuable with each one.

WHEN AUDITING EXISTING ENTRIES:
Walk them with the question "is this seed landing somewhere the LLM couldn't have reached on its own?" Some entries will rewrite themselves under that question. Pure restatements of common knowledge are byte cost without amplification benefit — delete or sharpen them.

CAVEAT:
This is a useful reframing, not a proven framework. Some entries (literal commands, version pins) are necessarily low-amplification and that's fine. Treat the principle as a lens, not a rule.

CARVE-OUT — context.next_session (v1.13.0 #91):
The seed-density principle does not apply to context.next_session. That key holds ephemeral state for cross-session handoff, not a seed to be activated — it's a short "here's where we left off" note the next session's bootstrap reads and acts on, then replaces. Apply this lens only to durable project knowledge; handoff entries are exempt by design.

See project.seedRoadmap for the operationalization plan (lint, topology analysis, north-star features).
